title: Building a Help Desk / Ticketing System in Laravel
subtitle: Ticket lifecycle, SLA timers, queues and assignment, email-to-ticket threading, canned replies, and escalation — the Laravel architecture behind a support desk.

sections.h2: How a help desk fits together

blocks.p: A help desk is a state machine wrapped around a conversation, governed by time. Two forces shape it: tickets move through a strict lifecycle (open → pending → resolved → closed), and SLA clocks tick against them, triggering escalation when breached. Everything else — threading, assignment, canned replies — serves those two.

blocks.diagram:
Customer ──email/web/chat──► Ticket
                                │
   ┌────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────┐
   ▼                            ▼                         ▼
 Lifecycle state          SLA policy clock          Conversation
 open→pending→resolved     first-response &           (threaded
 →closed (+reopen)         resolution timers          messages)
                                │
                                ▼  breach → escalate (notify, reassign)

blocks.p: The defining action — an agent replying — advances state, stops the response clock, and threads the message:

blocks.diagram:
Agent replies
   │ authorize (assigned/team)
   ▼
 Append Message (public)  ──► email customer (queued)
   │
   ▼ stop first-response SLA timer
 Set status = pending (awaiting customer)
   │
   ▼ log activity / update metrics

ul: Ticket lifecycle — an explicit, enforced state machine with allowed transitions.
SLA engine — first-response and resolution deadlines, business-hours aware, with breach escalation.
Conversation threading — public replies + internal notes, often fed by inbound email.
Routing — queues, teams, and assignment rules plus canned responses and macros.

blocks.callout: A help desk is a time-governed state machine over a conversation. The hard parts are correct SLA computation against business hours and reliable email-to-ticket threading.
blocks.title: The mental model in one sentence

blocks.p: A ticket has a status moving through defined states: open (needs agent), pending (awaiting customer), on-hold, resolved, closed, and reopened. Model transitions explicitly — only certain moves are legal (you can't close an open ticket without resolving). Enforce with a state-machine guard, not scattered ifs.

An SLA (Service Level Agreement) sets deadlines: first response within X, resolution within Y, varying by priority. The clock starts when the ticket is created (or assigned) and pauses while pending on the customer. Crucially, time is measured against business hours, not wall-clock — a ticket opened Friday 5pm isn't breached over the weekend.

You can't rely on a request to notice a breach — nobody may be looking. A scheduled job (every minute) scans tickets whose deadline has passed and fires escalation: notify a manager, raise priority, or reassign. Store the computed due_at on the ticket so the scan is a cheap indexed query, not a recomputation.

A ticket hasMany messages, each flagged public (visible to customer) or internal note (agents only). Order by time. The thread is the source of truth; the ticket row is a denormalized summary (status, last reply, counts).

Customers reply by email. You ingest via a mailbox webhook (Postmark/Mailgun/SES). The hard part is threading: match the reply to its ticket using a token in the address (support+T123@) or the In-Reply-To/References headers, strip quoted history, and append as a public message — creating a new ticket only if no match.
Tickets land in queues (by product/channel) and route to teams; assignment can be manual, round-robin, or skill/load-based. Keep assignment in a service and record who/when for reporting.
Canned responses are reusable reply templates; macros bundle actions (reply + set status + assign + tag) applied in one click. Both reduce handle time; store as data, render with ticket variables.

blocks.callout: Computing SLA against wall-clock instead of business hours is the classic help-desk bug — it falsely breaches tickets overnight/weekends. Always measure elapsed time within the customer's business calendar.
blocks.title: Most common misconception

blocks.p: A help desk moves tickets through an enforced lifecycle while business-hours SLA clocks tick toward escalation, with a threaded conversation fed by inbound email and sped up by canned replies.

Tickets are patients; the lifecycle is triage → treatment → discharge, and you can't discharge before treating (illegal transition). The SLA clock is the wait-time target — but it only counts while the department is open and the patient is actually waiting on staff, not while they're out getting a scan (pending). When a target is about to be missed, an alarm escalates to the charge nurse (manager). The thread is the patient chart: every note, in order, some visible to the patient, some staff-only.

Reliability
Effort
Note
rows: Standard but some clients drop them

blocks.diagram:
created ──[open]── agent replies ──[pending]── customer replies ──[open]── resolved
  │  clock RUNS         │  clock PAUSED        │  clock RUNS
  └───────── elapsed counted only while OPEN & within business hours ─────────┘

blocks.p: Each lesson is one idea. Code illustrative.

blocks.h3: Lesson 2 — Store computed due_at for cheap scans
Lesson 3 — Detect breaches on a schedule
Lesson 4 — Pause the clock when pending
Lesson 5 — Thread inbound email by token
Lesson 6 — Public reply vs internal note

blocks.code:
$ticket->messages()->create(['body'=>$text,'public'=>false]); // internal note
// public reply also emails the customer (queued) and stops the response clock

items.q: Q1. Why enforce ticket transitions with a state machine rather than free-form status updates?
items.a: Only certain moves are legal (e.g. you can't close without resolving, can't skip states). A central guard rejects illegal transitions consistently, instead of scattered conditionals that drift and allow invalid states.

items.q: Q2. Why must SLA elapsed time be measured against business hours?
items.a: Otherwise tickets falsely breach overnight and on weekends. Customers expect response within working time; the clock must only count hours the support team is actually operating.

items.q: Q3. Why store a computed due_at on the ticket instead of recomputing on each check?
items.a: Breach detection runs as a frequent scheduled scan; a stored due_at makes it a cheap indexed query (where due_at < now). Recomputing per ticket per scan is expensive and error-prone.

items.q: Q4. How does the SLA clock handle a ticket waiting on the customer?
items.a: It pauses while status is pending. Track pause intervals; net elapsed counts only running time, and the deadline is pushed forward by paused durations so customer delays don't count against the agent.

items.q: Q5. Why can't breach escalation rely on request-time checks?
items.a: A breach can occur when no one is interacting with the ticket (overnight, idle). A scheduled job must actively scan for passed deadlines and escalate; otherwise breaches go unnoticed.

items.q: Q6. What's the most reliable way to thread an inbound email reply to its ticket, and why?
items.a: A token in the reply address (support+T123@). It survives clients that strip In-Reply-To/References headers or edit the subject. Headers are a good secondary; subject tags are unreliable.

items.q: Q7. What is a macro and how does it differ from a canned response?
items.a: A canned response is a reusable reply template (text). A macro bundles multiple actions — reply + set status + assign + tag — applied in one click. Macros automate workflow, not just wording.

blocks.p: Re-do the quiz from memory; redraw the SLA pause diagram each session.

head: When
Session (~10–15 min)
Goal
rows: Read packet; quiz with answers open.
Quiz closed-book; redraw the lifecycle + legal transitions.
Interleaved quiz; write the breach-scan job from blank.
Teach SLA pausing to someone in 5 minutes.
Closed-book interleaved pass; misses restart Day 1.

blocks.callout: Retrieving just before you'd forget produces the strongest gain.
blocks.title: Why the gaps grow

blocks.p: Topics shuffled to train choosing the right idea.

items.q: M1. Tickets are breaching SLA over weekends though the team is closed. Root cause and fix?
items.a: SLA computed against wall-clock, not business hours. Fix: compute due_at and elapsed using a business calendar (working days/hours, holidays); pause outside operating time.

items.q: M2. Customer replies arrive as brand-new tickets instead of threading. Where do you look?
items.a: Inbound threading: the reply isn't being matched. Verify the address token (support+T123@) is present and parsed, fall back to In-Reply-To/References headers, and only open a new ticket when no match is found.

items.q: M3. The breach-escalation scan is slow at scale. Two fixes?
items.a: (1) Query on a stored, indexed due_at (where status=open and first_response_due_at < now) rather than recomputing. (2) Limit/chunk and only scan candidate statuses; consider per-priority partial indexes.

items.q: M4. An agent closed a ticket that was never resolved, skewing metrics. Prevention?
items.a: Enforce legal transitions in a state machine — closing requires passing through resolved. Free-form status edits allow invalid jumps; centralize and test the allowed map.

items.q: M5. SLA timer kept running while waiting on the customer, unfairly breaching agents. Fix?
items.a: Pause the clock on transition to pending and resume on reply; track pause intervals and extend due_at by paused duration so customer delay isn't counted.

items.q: M6. Support wants 'reply + set pending + assign to tier-2 + tag billing' in one click. What feature, and how stored?
items.a: A macro: stored as data (actions: reply template, target status, assignee, tags) and applied by an ApplyMacro service that runs each action, reusing the state machine for the status change.

ul: Build the SLA engine twice — wall-clock vs business-hours — and watch the false weekend breaches disappear.
Break threading on purpose — drop the address token and rely only on subject tags; reproduce the new-ticket-per-reply bug.
Write the transition tests first: every illegal move rejected, every legal move allowed.
Add CSAT — post-resolution satisfaction survey as a queued notification + metric.
Stretch goals: multi-channel (chat/social) into one inbox, round-robin with skills/load, holiday calendars per customer, and a reporting suite (first-response time, resolution time, reopen rate).

flashcards: The two hard parts of a help desk
Correct business-hours SLA computation and reliable email-to-ticket threading.
What a help desk fundamentally is
A time-governed state machine over a threaded conversation.
Why a state machine for status
Only certain transitions are legal; a central guard rejects illegal moves consistently.
The SLA wall-clock trap
Measuring elapsed time ignoring business hours falsely breaches tickets overnight/weekends.
Why store computed due_at
Makes the frequent breach scan a cheap indexed query instead of recomputation.
When the SLA clock pauses
While the ticket is pending on the customer; resume on their reply, extend due_at by paused time.
Why escalation needs a scheduled job
Breaches happen when no one's interacting; a per-minute scan actively finds passed deadlines.
Most reliable email threading
A token in the reply address (support+T123@); survives clients that strip headers.
Public message vs internal note
Public is visible to the customer (and emailed); internal note is agents-only.
Canned response vs macro
Canned = reusable reply text; macro = bundle of actions (reply+status+assign+tag) in one click.
Ticket row vs message thread
Thread is source of truth; ticket row is a denormalized summary (status, counts, last reply).
First-response vs resolution SLA
Two separate clocks: time to first agent reply, and time to resolve — often per-priority.
glossary: A support request with a status, priority, and conversation.
The set of statuses a ticket moves through (open→pending→resolved→closed).
A guard enforcing only legal status transitions.
Service Level Agreement: deadlines for response/resolution.
Elapsed time until the first agent reply.
The working calendar SLA elapsed time is measured against.
An SLA deadline passing without the required action.
Action taken on breach: notify, raise priority, reassign.
An agent-only message not shown to the customer.
Turning a received email into a threaded ticket message.
A reusable bundle of ticket actions applied together.
